text: The surgeon begins the type A radical hysterectomy by placing a primary umbilical 10 mm optical trocar and four secondary working ports. The surgical team stands on the left side of the patient, using ipsilateral ports. The dissection starts with opening the peritoneum below the right infundibulopelvic ligament, developing the rectovaginal space in the midline using a harmonic scalpel for precise dissection. The surgeon carefully opens the two layers of the Denonvilliers fascia, revealing the rectovaginal space. On the left side, the dissection of the left pararectal space is initiated, tracing the ureter and the division of the internal iliac vascular system. The uterine artery is identified at its origin and ligated using the harmonic scalpel's three-clip method. The deep uterine vein is noted at a deeper level, important for nerve-sparing procedures. The peritoneum is further opened to enlarge the spaces, with tenting movements of the Maryland grasper aiding in precise dissection.

The surgeon further develops the right pararectal space, then opens the round ligament near the pelvic side wall to access the retroperitoneum. The round ligament is cut parallel to the right infundibulopelvic ligament, which is skeletonized. A similar procedure is performed on the left side. The vesico-vaginal space is developed by extending the peritoneum cut anteriorly and lifting the fold to push the bladder caudally. The bladder pillar is taken at the cervix level. The infundibulopelvic ligament is ligated using a vessel sealing device, and the uterine artery is divided at the internal os.

The surgeon performs an extra facial hysterectomy without uterine manipulation, with assistant graspers holding the uterus. The para cervix is resected midway between the cervix and ureter, marked by an oblique red line. The vagina is opened, and a small three to five millimeter rim of vaginal tissue is excised around the cervix, maintaining a margin of less than one centimeter. The cervix is kept in view, and cuts are made into the vagina to include the entire cervix in the specimen. The procedure transitions to pelvic lymph node dissection, with the peritoneum fold hitched anteriorly to the abdominal wall for better lateral exposure.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the pelvic lymph nodes at the lateral border of the external iliac vascular system using a vessel sealing device. Small bursts of energy are applied to meticulously expose the external iliac vein, while the assistant grasper protects the vein laterally. The dissection is performed within defined limits: laterally by the genitofemoral nerve on the psoas muscle, medially by the ureter, inferiorly by the obturator nerve, caudally by the corona mortis, and superiorly by the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. The external iliac vein is bared, and the surgeon moves towards the ilio-obturator group of lymph nodes, ensuring to stay above the obturator nerve.

The surgeon identifies an enlarged node within the external iliac group of lymph nodes on the left side and carefully dissects around it to preserve the nodal tissue. The dissection proceeds towards the ilio obturator group of lymph nodes, with the obturator nerve visible at a deep level marked by a yellow arrow. The deep circumflex iliac vein, marked by oblique blue lines, is observed communicating between the external iliac vein and the obturator vein, forming the corona mortis. The assistant applies traction to the nodal mass, facilitating the division of lymphatics.

The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using a number one vicryl suture on a round body needle, employing an ipsilateral suturing technique. The nodal masses are placed into an endobag and extracted vaginally. The final view of the pelvis is shown after completing the pelvic lymphadenectomy.
